name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
الٓر ۚ كِتَـٰبٌ أَنزَلْنَـٰهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ ٱلنَّاسَ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ إِلَىٰ صِرَٰطِ ٱلْعَزِيزِ ٱلْحَمِيدِ (١)
```

```english
Alif, Lam, Ra. [This is] a Book We have sent down to you that you may bring mankind out from darkness into light, by the command of their Lord, to the path of the All-mighty, the All-laudable (1)
```

```arabic
ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِى لَهُۥ مَا فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَا فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ وَوَيْلٌ لِّلْكَـٰفِرِينَ مِنْ عَذَابٍ شَدِيدٍ (٢)
```

```english
—Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is on the earth. Woe to the faithless for a severe punishment (2)
```

```arabic
ٱلَّذِينَ يَسْتَحِبُّونَ ٱلْحَيَوٰةَ ٱلدُّنْيَا عَلَى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَن سَبِيلِ ٱللَّهِ وَيَبْغُونَهَا عِوَجًا ۚ أُو۟لَـٰٓئِكَ فِى ضَلَـٰلٍۭ بَعِيدٍ (٣)
```

```english
—those who prefer the life of this world to the Hereafter, and bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked. They are in extreme error. (3)
```

```arabic
وَمَآ أَرْسَلْنَا مِن رَّسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسَانِ قَوْمِهِۦ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ ۖ فَيُضِلُّ ٱللَّهُ مَن يَشَآءُ وَيَهْدِى مَن يَشَآءُ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْحَكِيمُ (٤)
```

```english
We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [Our messages] clear to them. Then Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and He guides whomsoever He wishes, and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise. (4)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَا مُوسَىٰ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَآ أَنْ أَخْرِجْ قَوْمَكَ مِنَ ٱلظُّلُمَـٰتِ إِلَى ٱلنُّورِ وَذَكِّرْهُم بِأَيَّىٰمِ ٱللَّهِ ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ (٥)
```

```english
Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs: ‘Bring your people out from darkness into light and remind them of Allah’s [holy] days. There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant].’ (5)
```

## (Commentary)

This noble Surah describes the Quran revealed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) insofar as it is the sign (آية) of his messengership, through which he brings people out of darkness into the light and guides them to the path of Allah, the Glorified, who is the All-Mighty (العزيز), the All-Laudable (الحميد). That is, He is the Victorious (الغالب) who is never overcome, the Self-Sufficient (الغني) who has no need of people, and the Beautiful (الجميل) in His actions, bestowing favors upon them. When the Bestower of favors is Victorious, Self-Sufficient, and Laudable in His acts, it is incumbent upon those favored to respond to His call and answer His summons so that they may find felicity through the blessings He pours upon them. They must also fear His wrath and His severe punishment, for He is Powerful and stands in need of no one; He has the right to dispense with them, doing away with them and bringing others in their stead, just as He did with the past nations who disbelieved in His favor. Indeed, the signs (آيات) of the heavens and the earth proclaim that all favor belongs to Him, and He is the Lord of Might and the Master of Praise; there is no lord besides Him.

With this, the Surah concludes, as He, the Mightiest of Speakers, says: **هذا بَلاغٌ لِلنَّاسِ وَلِيُنْذَرُوا بِهِ وَلِيَعْلَمُوا أَنَّما هُوَ إِلهٌ واحِدٌ وَلِيَذَّكَّرَ أُولُوا الْأَلْبابِ** — "This is a proclamation for mankind, so that they may be warned thereby, and know that He is indeed the One God, and so that those who possess intellect may take admonition." (14:52)

Perhaps what we have mentioned is the intent of those who said that the Surah opens by explaining the purpose of the messengership and the Book, referring to His saying, the Exalted: **لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ** — "...that you may bring mankind out of darkness into light by the leave of their Lord..." (14:1)

The Surah is Meccan, as indicated by the context of its verses. It has been attributed to Ibn Abbas, al-Hasan, and Qatadah that it is Meccan except for two verses revealed concerning the polytheists slain at Badr: **أَلَمْ تَرَ إِلَى الَّذِينَ بَدَّلُوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ كُفْراً وَأَحَلُّوا قَوْمَهُمْ دارَ الْبَوارِ جَهَنَّمَ يَصْلَوْنَها وَبِئْسَ الْقَرارُ** — "Have you not regarded those who substituted ungratefulness for Allah’s blessing, and settled their people in the abode of ruin? Hell, which they shall enter—an evil abiding place." (14:28-29). However, it will be shown later that these two verses are neither explicit nor apparent in that regard.

His saying, the Exalted: **الر كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ** — "Alif, Lam, Ra. [This is] a Book We have sent down to you that you may bring mankind out of darkness into light by the leave of their Lord." (14:1). That is: "This is a Book We have sent down to you." Thus, it is the predicate (خبر) of a suppressed subject (مبتدأ), as suggested by the context, though other views have been mentioned.

His saying: **لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ** — "...that you may bring mankind out of darkness into light." The apparent context implies all of mankind, not specifically his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) people, nor specifically the believers among them, as there is no linguistic evidence for such restriction. His saying, the Exalted, is explicit regarding the universality of the messengership, such as His saying: **لِيَكُونَ لِلْعالَمِينَ نَذِيراً** — "...that he may be a warner to all the worlds" (25:1), and His saying: **لِأُنْذِرَكُمْ بِهِ وَمَنْ بَلَغَ** — "...that I may warn you thereby and whomever it may reach" (6:19), and His saying: **قُلْ يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنِّي رَسُولُ اللهِ إِلَيْكُمْ جَمِيعاً** — "Say, ‘O mankind! I am indeed the Apostle of Allah to you all’" (7:158). Furthermore, the verses explicitly calling the Jews and the People of the Book in general, and his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) actions in inviting them and accepting the faith of those who believed—such as Abdullah ibn Salam, Salman, Bilal, Suhayb, and others—support this.

Moreover, the end of the Surah: **هذا بَلاغٌ لِلنَّاسِ وَلِيُنْذَرُوا بِهِ** — "This is a proclamation for mankind, so that they may be warned thereby" (14:52), which corresponds to its beginning, supports the view that "mankind" (الناس) is broader than just the believers who have actually come out of darkness into the light.

The act of bringing out from darkness into light is attributed to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) because he is one of the apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) for it; the faith of the believers traces back to his call, whether directly or through an intermediary. This does not contradict His saying: **إِنَّكَ لا تَهْدِي مَنْ أَحْبَبْتَ وَلكِنَّ اللهَ يَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ** — "You cannot guide whomever you wish, but [it is] Allah [who] guides whomever He wishes" (28:56), for that verse only denies his (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) essentiality (أصالة) and independence (استقلال) in guidance, without denying him absolute guidance, even that which occurs by way of mediation and by the leave of Allah. The proof for this is His saying, the Exalted: **وَإِنَّكَ لَتَهْدِي إِلى صِراطٍ مُسْتَقِيمٍ** — "And indeed you guide to a straight path" (42:52). For this reason, the Glorified restricted His saying **لِتُخْرِجَ** (that you may bring out) with the phrase **بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ** (by the leave of their Lord).

Regarding the meaning of darkness (الظلمات), light (النور), misguidance (الضلال), and guidance (الهدى) — *the divine leading toward truth and the straight path* — it is frequently repeated in His speech, the Exalted, that guidance is considered light and misguidance is counted as darkness. "Darkness" is mentioned in the plural while "light" is singular because guidance stems from the Truth, and the Truth is one; there is no variation between its parts or instances, nor is there multiplicity. This is in contrast to misguidance, which stems from following whims (الهوى), and whims are diverse and conflicting, possessing no unity or cohesion between their parts or instances. The Exalted said: **وَأَنَّ هذا صِراطِي مُسْتَقِيماً فَاتَّبِعُوهُ وَلا تَتَّبِعُوا السُّبُلَ فَتَفَرَّقَ بِكُمْ عَنْ سَبِيلِهِ** — "This is My straight path, so follow it, and do not follow [other] ways, for they will separate you from His way." (6:153).

The "Lam" in His saying: **لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ** (that you may bring mankind out) etc., is the "Lam" of purpose (لام الغرض) based on the universality of "mankind," as is apparent from the verse. It is not the "Lam" of consequence (لام المعاقبة), for if it were so, all people would have become believers, and the known reality is otherwise.

As for the objection raised by some that divine nurturing—by bringing people out of darkness into light and leading them to felicity and perfection—is conditioned upon readiness and receptivity (التهيؤ والاستعداد) despite the grace (الفيض) being universal, and thus the possible extent of this consequence, assuming its universality, is only this much—

The response is that this is an admission that taking the "Lam" as one of consequence contradicts the apparent meaning of the verse. What they mentioned cannot be established except by restricting "mankind" (الناس) to those who are "ready" (المستعدين). However, what must be known is that this purpose is a legislative purpose (غرض تشريعي), meaning that the ruling has an intended goal, which is the benefit (المصلحة) that follows it. Indeed, Allah, the Glorified, calls people so that He may forgive them and guide them to faith and righteous deeds (الصالحات), thereby making them felicitous and entering them into Paradise. He sends messengers and reveals the Book to them so that they may bring people out of darkness into light by the leave of their Lord. He intends, through the commands and prohibitions He directs toward them, to purify them and remove from them the defilement of Satan. The verses indicating this are many, and there is no need to cite them all, as are the narrations, which perhaps number in the thousands.

The Glorified has said: **إِنَّا جَعَلْناهُ قُرْآناً عَرَبِيًّا لَعَلَّكُمْ تَعْقِلُونَ** — "We have made it an Arabic Quran so that you may exercise your reason." (43:3), and He said: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ فَيُضِلُّ اللهُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ** — "We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [things] clear to them. Then Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and guides whomever He wishes..." (14:4). Thus, He explained that what we understand from His Book and what becomes clear to us from the explanation of His Messenger is a binding proof (حجة) from which there is no escape. We do not understand from His saying, for example: **يَدْعُوكُمْ لِيَغْفِرَ لَكُمْ** — "He calls you that He may forgive you" (14:10), except that forgiveness is the purpose of the call. Similarly, we do not understand from the words of a master to his servant, or any leader to his follower: "Bring me water so that I may drink," or "food so that I may eat," or "clothe so-and-so so that he may cover his nakedness," except that drinking, eating, and covering are the purposes of these commands. Thus, for Allah, the Glorified, there are intended purposes and goals in the rulings and laws He reveals.

Yes, the Glorified has explained that His presence is transcendent above poverty and need, and free from deficiency or blemish, as He said: **إِنَّ اللهَ لَغَنِيٌّ عَنِ الْعالَمِينَ** — "Indeed Allah is independent of all the worlds." (29:6), and He said: **وَرَبُّكَ الْغَنِيُّ ذُو الرَّحْمَةِ** — "Your Lord is the Self-sufficient One, the Lord of mercy." (6:133), and He said: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ أَنْتُمُ الْفُقَراءُ إِلَى اللهِ وَاللهُ هُوَ الْغَنِيُّ** — "O mankind! You are the ones who stand in need of Allah, and Allah—He is the All-sufficient, the All-laudable." (35:15). Thus, He informed that He is independent of everything; He does not benefit from any of these purposes. Yet, the acts of the Exalted are not in vain or haphazard (العبث والجزاف) such that they would be devoid of purpose. How could they be, when He has described Himself as the All-Wise (الحكيم), and the wise one does not act in vain or haphazardly? He explicitly negated vanity from His action: **أَفَحَسِبْتُمْ أَنَّما خَلَقْناكُمْ عَبَثاً** — "Did you suppose that We created you aimlessly..." (23:115). The command and prohibition through which perfection is achieved in the human world ultimately return to that which completes the creation.

Thus, for Allah, the Glorified, there are purposes in His creation and His command, even if He does not attain perfection through the purposes of His acts as we attain perfection through the purposes of ours. He, the Glorified, is not affected by His purposes. In other words, wisdoms and benefits (المصالح) do not affect Him, the Exalted, just as the benefit of an act affects us, where our conceptualization of it motivates us toward the act and makes us prefer acting over refraining. For He, the Glorified, is the Subduer (القاهر) who is never subdued, and the Victorious (الغالب) who is never overcome. He owns everything and nothing owns Him; He rules over everything and nothing rules over Him. He has no partner in sovereignty, nor any ally out of weakness. Thus, He, the Exalted, is not governed by reason; rather, He is the one who guides reason to what it conceptualizes. No benefit (مصلحة) compels Him to act, nor does any harm (مفسدة) compel Him to refrain; rather, He is the Guide for both toward what they necessitate.

Therefore, the purpose and benefit are abstracted from the station of His action (مقام فعله), in the sense that His action depends on the benefit, but the benefit does not govern His Essence (الذات), the Exalted, nor does it compel Him to act. Just as when He, the Exalted, creates something and says to it, "Be," and it is—such as Zayd, for instance—the intellect abstracts from the external reality itself that it is an origination (إيجاد) from Allah and an existence (وجود) for Zayd, and it judges that his existence depends on His origination. Similarly, the intellect abstracts from the act of the Exalted—in view of His supreme attributes that we have alluded to—that it is His act and that it possesses an intended benefit, and then it judges that the realization of the act depends on its possessing a benefit.

This is what reflection upon His speech, the Exalted, yields regarding His acts being comprised of wisdoms and benefits and depending on purposes. The result is that He, the Exalted, has purposes in His acts, but they return to His creation rather than to Himself.

In summary, His purpose in His act differs from our purposes in our acts in two ways: first, He, the Exalted, does not attain perfection through the purposes and goals of His acts, unlike us—the community of conscious and intentional beings among humans and other animals. Second, benefit and harm do not govern Him, the Exalted, unlike others.

As for the well-known dispute between the Ash'arites and the Mu'tazilites regarding whether the acts of Allah are caused by purposes or not—in the sense of whether He, the Exalted, is governed by objective benefit (المصلحة الواقعية) in His act such that the benefit makes the act preferable to Him over refraining, and without it He would not have acted? Or that He has no goal in His act and only acts by a haphazard will without purpose?

Neither side of this dispute leads to anything through exhaustive investigation. The truth is contrary to both views; it is a matter between the two matters (أمر بين الأمرين), as we have indicated. Perhaps we shall be successful in the future parts of this book to dedicate an independent discussion to this issue, exhausting the rational and traditional investigation therein, if Allah, the Exalted, wills.

In His saying: **بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ** (by the leave of their Lord), there is a transition (التفات) from the first-person plural ("We") to the third person. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to transition to the mention of the attribute of Lordship (الربوبية) and to establish that He, the Exalted, is the Lord of these polytheists who have taken peers alongside Him. For the discourse is, in reality, directed toward them, even if the one addressed is the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) rather than them. Furthermore, this naming at the opening of the discourse serves as a starting point for the arguments for the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية) that will be mentioned in the Surah.

His saying, the Exalted: **إِلى صِراطِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ اللهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "...to the path of the All-mighty, the All-laudable, Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth." (14:1-2). Might (العزة) is the opposite of humiliation. Al-Raghib said: "Might is a state that prevents a person from being overcome, from their saying: 'tough ground' (أرض عزاز), meaning hard. The Exalted said: **أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِنْدَهُمُ الْعِزةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً** — 'Do they seek might with them? Indeed, all might belongs to Allah.' (4:139). And 'the meat became tough' (تعزز اللحم) means it hardened, and 'it became mighty' (عز) as if it became situated in a hard place (عزاز) difficult to reach." End of the relevant portion.

Thus, the might of the All-Mighty (العزيز) is His being such that it is difficult to reach or overcome Him. From this comes "the mighty one of the people," who subdues and is not subdued, because he possesses a station that no one who seeks him can reach without being prevented and subdued before reaching him. From this also comes "mighty" in the sense of rare, because of the difficulty of obtaining it. And from it comes "mighty" in the sense of distressing, because that which is distressing to a person is difficult to attain; the Exalted said: **عَزِيزٌ عَلَيْهِ ما عَنِتُّمْ** — "...grievous to him is your distress" (9:128). And from it is His saying: **وَعَزَّنِي فِي الْخِطابِ** — "...and he has got the better of me in speech" (38:23), meaning he overcame me, according to one interpretation.

Allah, the Glorified, is All-Mighty (عزيز) because He is the Essence (الذات) whom nothing overcomes from any direction, while He overcomes everything from every direction. Therefore, might is restricted to Him, the Exalted; it is not found with anyone else except through acquisition from Him and by His leave. The Exalted said: **أَيَبْتَغُونَ عِنْدَهُمُ الْعِزَّةَ فَإِنَّ الْعِزَّةَ لِلَّهِ جَمِيعاً** — "Do they seek might with them? Indeed, all might belongs to Allah" (4:139), and He said: **مَنْ كانَ يُرِيدُ الْعِزَّةَ فَلِلَّهِ الْعِزَّةُ جَمِيعاً** — "Whoever seeks might—to Allah belongs all might" (35:10).

The All-Laudable (الحميد) is in the form of *fa'il* with the meaning of the passive participle (مفعول), from praise (الحمد), which is the commendation of a beautiful voluntary act. Since every beauty traces back to Him, the Glorified, all praise belongs to Him, as He said: **الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ** — "All praise belongs to Allah, Lord of all the worlds" (1:2). A strange view is that reported from Imam al-Razi, which we shall quote: that "the All-Laudable" (الحميد) means the All-Knowing, the Self-Sufficient.

His saying: **إِلى صِراطِ الْعَزِيزِ الْحَمِيدِ** — "...to the path of the All-mighty, the All-laudable," is an appositive (بدل) for His saying: **إِلَى النُّورِ** (to the light). Through it, He explains what the Book He revealed to His Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) leads to, providing one explanation after another. He first pointed out that it is a light that distinguishes truth from falsehood, good from evil, and felicity from wretchedness. Second, He pointed out that it is a clear path (صراط) that gathers its travelers upon its surface and leads them all to Allah, the All-Mighty, the All-Laudable.

The reason for mentioning these two noble attributes, "the All-Mighty" and "the All-Laudable," is that they are the principles for the discourse that will be directed toward them in the Surah. For the core of the discourse in the Surah is to remind them that Allah has bestowed upon them every great blessing through His Lordship. Then He commanded them through His messengers to thank Him and not be ungrateful. He promised His messengers that if they believed, He would enter them into Paradise, and if they disbelieved, He would take vengeance upon them and bring them to the place of wretchedness and punishment. Thus, they should fear their Lord and beware of opposing His command and being ungrateful for His blessing, because to Him belongs all might—nothing prevents His wrath from befalling them or His punishment from descending upon them—and He is All-Laudable, not to be blamed for rewarding the believers, nor for punishing the disbelievers, just as He is not to be blamed for the countless blessings He has spread over them.

Thus, the bulk of the discourse in this Surah concerns what these three attributes necessitate: His uniqueness in Lordship, His Might, and His being Laudable in His acts. Therefore, one should fear His absolute Might, give thanks, trust in what He has promised, and take admonition from the signs of His Lordship.

In *Ruh al-Ma'ani*, quoted from Abu Hayyan: "The subtle point in this is that since He previously mentioned the revelation of this Book and the bringing of people out of darkness into light by the leave of their Lord, it was appropriate to mention these two attributes: the attribute of Might, which implies power and victory, for the revelation of such a miraculous Book that none but He is capable of; and the attribute of Praise, for bestowing the greatest of blessings by bringing people out of darkness into light." He said: "The reason for the sequence of the two is thus clear." End quote.

This is entirely foreign to the context of the Surah's verses. Perhaps it is taken from His saying, the Exalted, in describing the Quran: **وَإِنَّهُ لَكِتابٌ عَزِيزٌ لا يَأْتِيهِ الْباطِلُ مِنْ بَيْنِ يَدَيْهِ وَلا مِنْ خَلْفِهِ تَنْزِيلٌ مِنْ حَكِيمٍ حَمِيدٍ** — "Indeed it is an august Book: falsehood cannot approach it from any direction, [it is] a successive revelation from One All-wise, All-laudable." (41:41-42). However, the context here is different.

It is narrated from the Imam [al-Razi] in his *Tafsir*: "The mention of the All-Mighty was placed before the All-Laudable because the correct view is that the first knowledge of Allah, the Exalted, is the knowledge of His being Powerful, then after that the knowledge of His being All-Knowing, then after that the knowledge of His being independent of needs. The All-Mighty is the Powerful, and the All-Laudable is the All-Knowing, the Self-Sufficient. Since the knowledge of His being Powerful precedes the knowledge of His being All-Knowing of everything and independent of it, the mention of the All-Mighty was necessarily placed before the All-Laudable." End quote. This is a remarkably haphazard claim.

Similar in its haphazardness is the saying of some: "The All-Mighty was placed before the All-Laudable out of concern for the negative attributes (الصفات السلبية), as suggested by the saying: 'Emptying is prior to adorning' (التخلية أولى من التحلية), for Might—as previously mentioned—is among the negative attributes, unlike Praise."

It has also been said that the reason for specifying these two attributes for mention is to encourage traveling this path, because it is the path of the All-Mighty, the All-Laudable; thus, its traveler becomes mighty and its wayfarer becomes lauded. End quote. This is a point more suited to be among the derived benefits rather than the necessitating cause. The correct reason is what we have previously stated.

As for His saying: **اللهِ الَّذِي لَهُ ما فِي السَّماواتِ وَما فِي الْأَرْضِ** — "...Allah, to whom belongs whatever is in the heavens and whatever is in the earth" (14:2), it is an explanation for "the All-Mighty, the All-Laudable." What is meant by "whatever is in the heavens and the earth" is everything in existence; it includes the heavens and the earth themselves just as it includes what is within them. Thus, He, the Exalted, owns everything from every direction in the true sense of ownership (الملك).

In this is an indication of the proof for His being All-Mighty and All-Laudable. For He, the Exalted, although He is the one who establishes the truth by His words and the one who makes every proof successful in its indication, He nonetheless addresses His servants in His speech according to the primordial nature (الفطرة) — *the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being* — upon which He created them. That is, since He owns every creation and command in the true sense of ownership, He is the owner of every power and victory; there is no power except from Him and no victory except for Him. Thus, He, the Exalted, is All-Mighty, and He has the right to dispose of whatever He wills however He wills. His disposal can only be laudable and never blameworthy, for disposal is only blameworthy if the disposer does not own it, whether rationally, legally, or conventionally. Any disposal that reason, law, or convention attributes to Him, the Exalted, He owns it. Thus, He, the Exalted, is All-Laudable, lauded in His actions.

His saying, the Exalted: **وَوَيْلٌ لِلْكافِرِينَ مِنْ عَذابٍ شَدِيدٍ** — "...and woe to the faithless for a severe punishment" (14:2), is an explanation of what the attribute of Might necessitates of subdual for those who reject His call and are ungrateful for His blessing.

His saying, the Exalted: **الَّذِينَ يَسْتَحِبُّونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا عَلَى الْآخِرَةِ وَيَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً** — "...those who prefer the life of this world to the Hereafter, and bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked..." (14:3). Al-Raghib said in *al-Mufradat*: "And His saying, the Mighty and Majestic: **إِنِ اسْتَحَبُّوا الْكُفْرَ عَلَى الْإِيمانِ** — '...if they prefer unfaith to faith' (9:23), means if they choose it over it. The reality of 'preferring' (الاستحباب) is for a person to seek to love something, and its being made transitive with 'over' (على) implies the meaning of preference (الإيثار). Based on this is His saying, the Exalted: **وَأَمَّا ثَمُودُ فَهَدَيْناهُمْ فَاسْتَحَبُّوا الْعَمى عَلَى الْهُدى** — 'As for [the people of] Thamud, We guided them, but they preferred blindness to guidance' (41:17)." End quote.

The meaning of preferring the world over the Hereafter is choosing the world and abandoning the Hereafter entirely. Its opposite is choosing the Hereafter over the world, in the sense of taking the Hereafter as the goal of one's striving and making the world a means (مقدمة) to it, through which one reaches it. As for choosing the Hereafter and abandoning the world entirely, besides being impossible in the true sense of the word, it would lead to the disruption of the Hereafter's affairs and result in its ultimate abandonment. For the worldly life is a finite life, and the afterlife is an eternal life, the felicity of which is attained through the world by acquisition. Thus, whoever chooses the Hereafter and affirms it must necessarily affirm the world because of its status as a means. Whoever chooses the world and makes it the goal must necessarily negate the Hereafter entirely, for if it were affirmed, it would be affirmed as a goal, and since it is not made a goal, it is negated. Thus, there are only two paths before man: choosing the Hereafter over the world by making the Hereafter the goal and affirming the world with it as a means, or choosing the world over the Hereafter by making the world the goal and negating the Hereafter entirely.

To clarify the matter: man has no desire except for the felicity of his life, and his love for it is primordial (فطري). We have explained this in various places previously. What the Book of Allah affirms regarding the matter of life is that it is eternal, not cut off by death. Therefore, it necessarily divides, in view of the intervention of death, into two lives: the worldly life (الحياة الدنيا) timed by death, and the afterlife (الحياة الآخرة) after death. The latter's felicity or wretchedness stems from the worldly life and what man acquires in the world through vital actions, whether good or evil. Man cannot escape these actions because of his primordial love for life.

These actions—that is, the way (السنة) that man follows in his worldly life, which earns him godwariness (التقوى) or profligacy (الفجور), and good or evil—are what is called in the Book of Allah "religion" (دين) and "way" (سبيل). Thus, man cannot escape having a way, whether good or evil, and a religion, whether true or false.

Since it is among the established ways of Allah, the Glorified, to guide every species to its felicity and perfection, and since it is part of man's perfection and felicity to live a social life and follow a vital way, Allah, the Glorified, has legislated for him a religion based on the primordial nature (الفطرة) upon which He created him. This is the way of Allah (سبيل الله) which he travels and His religion which he professes. If he proceeds according to what Lordship has legislated for him and what the primordial nature has guided him to, then he has traveled the way of Allah and sought it straight. But if he follows whims and bars himself from the way of Allah and occupies himself with what Satan makes fair-seeming to him, then he has sought the way of Allah as something crooked and deviated.

As for his seeking the way of Allah, it is because Allah is the one who fashioned him upon the seeking of a way and the pursuit of a path, and He guides only to what He is pleased with, which is His own way. As for its being deviated and crooked, it is because he does not guide to the Truth, and what is there after the Truth except misguidance? The Quranic verses indicating this which we have presented are numerous; there is no need to cite them.

Once you know this, it becomes clear to you that His saying in explaining the faithless: **الَّذِينَ يَسْتَحِبُّونَ الْحَياةَ الدُّنْيا عَلَى الْآخِرَةِ** — "...those who prefer the life of this world to the Hereafter," means that they are completely attached to the worldly life and turn away from the Hereafter by negating it. This is disbelief in the Return (المعاد), which necessitates disbelief in Monotheism (التوحيد) and Prophethood (النبوة).

His saying: **وَيَصُدُّونَ عَنْ سَبِيلِ اللهِ وَيَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً** — "...and bar [others] from the way of Allah, and seek to make it crooked," means that they restrain themselves from following the way of Allah and professing His religion, or they bar and turn people away from faith in Allah and the Last Day and from practicing His Sharia out of their enmity toward the Truth. They seek the way of Allah as something crooked and deviated by following other social ways, whatever they may be. Then He recorded their misguidance by His saying, the Glorified: **أُولئِكَ فِي ضَلالٍ بَعِيدٍ** — "...They are in extreme error." (14:3).

From what has preceded, the invalidity of the view of some becomes apparent, who said that the meaning of **يَبْغُونَها عِوَجاً** is that they seek for it (the way) crookedness, meaning they seek for it deviation and distortion so that they may find fault with it and bar people from it because of that.

Also, the view of some who said: the meaning is they seek to see crookedness in it that would be a defect, so they may disparage it thereby.

And the view of some who said: the meaning is they seek for its people to become crooked and deviate by apostasy; thus, this is what is meant by their seeking the religion to be deviated, and its deviation is the corruption of the knowledge the believers possess. The corruption of these views is evident.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ** — "We did not send any apostle except with the language of his people, so that he might make [things] clear to them..." (14:4) to the end of the verse. "Language" (اللسان) is the tongue. The Exalted said: **بِلِسانٍ عَرَبِيٍّ مُبِينٍ** — "In a clear Arabic language." (26:195).

The pronoun in "his people" (قومه) refers back to "apostle" (رسول), and in "to them" (لهم) to "his people." The sum of it is: We did not send any apostle except with the language of the people of that apostle, so that he might make things clear to his people. It is an error to return the pronoun of "his people" to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) to imply that Allah, the Glorified, used to reveal to all messengers in Arabic, for the meaning would be corrupted by that, as the pronoun "to them" (لهم) would return to "his people," implying that Allah revealed the Torah to Moses, for example, in Arabic to make things clear to the Arabs, as mentioned in *al-Kashshaf*.

What is meant by sending the messenger with the language of his people is sending him with the language of the people among whom he lived, mixed, and socialized. It does not mean sending him with the language of the people from whom he descends by lineage. For the Glorified explicitly mentions the migration of Lot (peace be upon him) from Chaldea—and they were of Syriac tongue—to the Overturned Cities (المؤتفكات), who were Hebrews, yet He called them "his people" and sent him to them, then saved him and his family except his wife, who was from them, and destroyed them. The Exalted said: **فَآمَنَ لَهُ لُوطٌ وَقالَ إِنِّي مُهاجِرٌ إِلى رَبِّي** — "So Lot believed in him, and he said, ‘I am indeed migrating toward my Lord’" (29:26), and in various places in His speech: "the people of Lot" (قوم لوط).

As for those sent to more than one nation—and they are the Arch-Prophets (أولوا العزم) — *the messengers of firm resolve* — among the messengers—the evidence that they used to call nations other than those of their own tongue includes what Allah narrated of Abraham's (peace be upon him) call to the Arabs of Hijaz to the Hajj, and Moses' (peace be upon him) call to Pharaoh and his people to faith, and the universality of the Prophet's (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) call. The Quran includes the call to the Jews, Christians, and others, and the acceptance of the faith of those who believed in the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم). Likewise, what is understood from the universality of Noah's (peace be upon him) call. Accordingly, the meaning of His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ** —and Allah knows best—is that Allah did not base the sending of messengers and the religious call upon a miraculous basis that breaks the normal course of things, nor did He delegate any of the matter to His messengers. Rather, He sent them with the ordinary language with which they spoke to their people and conversed with them, so that they might clarify the purposes of revelation to them. They have nothing but the clarification (البيان). As for what lies beyond that of guidance and misguidance, it belongs to Allah, the Glorified; no messenger or anyone else shares with Him in that.

Thus, the verse becomes like an explanation and clarification for His saying, the Exalted, earlier: **كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ** — "[This is] a Book We have sent down to you that you may bring mankind out of darkness into light by the leave of their Lord" (14:1). The meaning of your bringing people out of darkness into light is that you clarify to them what Allah has revealed, nothing more than that. Thus, it carries the meaning of His saying: **وَأَنْزَلْنا إِلَيْكَ الذِّكْرَ لِتُبَيِّنَ لِلنَّاسِ ما نُزِّلَ إِلَيْهِمْ** — "...and We have sent down the Reminder to you so that you may clarify to the people what has been sent down to them" (16:44).

As for His saying: **فَيُضِلُّ اللهُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ** — "...Then Allah leads astray whomever He wishes, and guides whomever He wishes" (14:4), it is an indication of what we alluded to: that the matter of guidance and misguidance belongs to Allah; neither of them is realized except by a will from Him, the Exalted. However, the Glorified has informed us that this will of His is not haphazard or disorganized; rather, it has a fixed order. Whoever follows the Truth and does not oppose it, Allah guides him. And whoever rejects it and follows his whims, Allah leads him astray. This is a misguidance of recompense (إضلال مجازاة), which is different from the blameworthy initial misguidance (الإضلال الابتدائي).

The Glorified placed misguidance before guidance when He said: **فَيُضِلُّ اللهُ مَنْ يَشاءُ وَيَهْدِي مَنْ يَشاءُ** because that is more in need of clarification, given that the discourse is built upon His absolute Might. It was necessary to explain that the misguidance of those who stray from the path, like the guidance of those who are guided to it, is only by a will from Him, the Exalted. He is not overcome in His intention, nor is He contested in His sovereignty. This is so that it does not occur to any heedless person that Allah describes Himself with absolute Might and that He is the Victorious who is never overcome and the Subduer who is never subdued, yet He calls people and they do not respond to His call, and He commands and prohibits them and they disobey and do not obey—is this not a victory on their part and a subdual, while He is overcome and subdued?

It is as if He, the Exalted, answered that by saying that the meaning of His call, the Exalted, is that He sends a messenger with the language of his people to clarify to them what makes them felicitous and what makes them wretched. As for the misguidance of those who stray among people, like the guidance of those who are guided among them, it is by the will and leave of Allah. Far be it from Him that anyone should subdue Him in His authority or dispose in His kingdom without His leave.

Thus, the misguidance of those who stray among them is a proof of His Might, let alone being a negation of it, just as the guidance of those who are guided is likewise. For this reason, He concluded the speech with His saying: **وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْحَكِيمُ** — "...and He is the All-mighty, the All-wise." (14:4). He, the Glorified, is All-Mighty; the misguidance of those who stray does not overcome Him nor harm Him, nor does the guidance of those who are guided benefit Him. He is All-Wise; He does not will what He wills haphazardly or in vain, but rather according to a firm and permanent order.

**His saying, the Exalted**: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مُوسى بِآياتِنا أَنْ أَخْرِجْ قَوْمَكَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ** — "Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs: ‘Bring your people out of darkness into light...’" (14:5) to the end of the verse. Since the discourse in the Surah is built upon warning and reminding of the Might of Allah, the Glorified, it was appropriate to mention the sending of Moses with signs to guide his people. For the story of his messengership is among the clearest instances of the manifestation of divine Might among the messengers. The Exalted said regarding him: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مُوسى بِآياتِنا وَسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ** — "Certainly We sent Moses with Our signs and a manifest authority" (40:23), and He said, narrating from him (peace be upon him): **وَأَنْ لا تَعْلُوا عَلَى اللهِ إِنِّي آتِيكُمْ بِسُلْطانٍ مُبِينٍ** — "and ‘Do not defy Allah. Indeed I bring you a manifest authority’" (44:19).

The weight of this verse—that is, His saying: **وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا مُوسى بِآياتِنا أَنْ أَخْرِجْ قَوْمَكَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ**—relative to His saying: **كِتابٌ أَنْزَلْناهُ إِلَيْكَ لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ مِنَ الظُّلُماتِ إِلَى النُّورِ بِإِذْنِ رَبِّهِمْ**, is the weight of an analogy (التنظير) for the purpose of support and heartening, as in His saying: **إِنَّا أَوْحَيْنا إِلَيْكَ كَما أَوْحَيْنا إِلى نُوحٍ وَالنَّبِيِّينَ مِنْ بَعْدِهِ** — "We have indeed revealed to you as We revealed to Noah and the prophets after him..." (4:163).

As for what some have mentioned, that the verse is a beginning of the detailing of what was summarized in His saying: **وَما أَرْسَلْنا مِنْ رَسُولٍ إِلَّا بِلِسانِ قَوْمِهِ لِيُبَيِّنَ لَهُمْ**, it is extremely far-fetched. Equally far-fetched is the saying of some that the "signs" (الآيات) with which Moses was sent are the verses of the Torah, rather than the miracles with which he (peace be upon him) was sent, such as the serpent, the white hand, and others.

Furthermore, Allah, the Glorified and Exalted, never counted the Torah in His speech among the signs of Moses' messengership, nor did He ever mention that He sent him with it. He only mentioned that He revealed it to him and gave it to him.

He did not restrict His saying: **أَنْ أَخْرِجْ قَوْمَكَ** (Bring your people out) etc., with "the leave," as He restricted His saying to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) **لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ** etc., because His saying here: **أَخْرِجْ قَوْمَكَ** is a command that implies the meaning of leave, unlike His saying there: **لِتُخْرِجَ النَّاسَ**.

His saying: **وَذَكِّرْهُمْ بِأَيَّامِ اللهِ** — "...and remind them of Allah’s days" (14:5). There is no doubt that what is meant by them are specific days. The attribution of specific days to Allah, the Glorified—despite all days and all things belonging to Him, the Exalted—is only because of the manifestation of His command therein, a manifestation with which no manifestation remains for anyone else. They are the times and circumstances in which His command, the signs of His oneness, and His sovereignty have appeared or will appear. Such as the day of death, in which the sovereignty of the Hereafter appears and worldly causes cease to have effect; and the Day of Resurrection, in which no soul shall have power to do anything for another soul, and the command that day belongs to Allah. And like the days in which Allah destroyed the people of Noah, 'Ad, and Thamud. For these and their like are days in which divine victory and subdual appeared, and that all might belongs to Allah.

It is possible that among them are days in which divine blessings appeared so clearly that no one besides Him, the Exalted, had any hand in them, such as the day of the exit of Noah (peace be upon him) and his companions from the Ark with peace from Allah and blessings, and the day of Abraham's rescue from the fire, and others. For these also, like the preceding ones, have no real attribution to anyone besides Him, the Exalted. Thus, they are the "days of Allah" attributed to Him, just as days are attributed to nations and peoples; from this are the "days of the Arabs," such as the Day of Dhi Qar, the Day of Fijar, the Day of Bu'ath, and others.

The restriction by some of "the days" to the favors (نعماء) of Allah, the Glorified, in view of the subsequent mention of His blessings, like the restriction by others to His punishments (نقمات), is devoid of basis, given that the discourse in the Surah proceeds according to what His Might necessitates. Among the requirements of the attribute of Might is bestowing favors upon the servants and the severe seizing if they are ungrateful for His blessing.

Then He completed the speech with His saying: **إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِكُلِّ صَبَّارٍ شَكُورٍ** — "...There are indeed signs in that for every patient and grateful [servant]." (14:5). That is, one who is very patient (صبار) during hardship and very grateful (شكور) for blessings.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Durr al-Manthur*, Ahmad narrated from Abu Dharr, who said: The Messenger of Allah (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) said: "Allah never sent a prophet except with the language of his people."

In the same source, al-Nasa'i, Abdullah ibn Ahmad in *Zawa'id al-Musnad*, ibn Jarir, ibn al-Mundhir, ibn Abi Hatim, ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in *Shu'ab al-Iman* narrated from Ubayy ibn Ka'b, from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم) regarding His saying: **وَذَكِّرْهُمْ بِأَيَّامِ اللهِ** (and remind them of Allah’s days). He said: "With the blessings of Allah and His favors."

I say: This is an explanation of some of the instances. Al-Tabarsi and al-Ayyashi narrated something similar in meaning from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام).

In the *Amali* of the Shaykh [al-Tusi], with his chain of narration from Abdullah ibn Abbas and Jabir ibn Abdullah, in a long hadith from the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله وسلم): "The days of Allah are His blessings, His trials, and His exemplary punishments (مثلاته), the Glorified."

In the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, he said: He said: "The days of Allah are three: the day of the Riser (القائم), the day of death, and the Day of Resurrection."

I say: The intent is to explain His great days, not to restrict the totality of His days.

In *al-Ma'ani*, with its chain of narration from Muthanna al-Hannat from Abu Ja'far and Abu Abdullah (عليهما السلام), they said: "The days of Allah are three: the day the Riser (القائم) rises, the day of the Return (الكرة), and the Day of Resurrection."

I say: These are like the preceding ones, and the variation in the narrations regarding the number of instances supports what we presented in the explanation of the verse.
